structured.title: Genre Shapes Psalm Interpretation
structured.overview: The speaker explains that Psalms should not be read as a rigid, one-size-fits-all text. Instead, they should be interpreted through genre and “family resemblances,” meaning recognizable patterns that shape expectations and help readers understand meaning accurately. The discussion emphasizes that different types of biblical writing use structure, variation, and imitation, and that missing genre can lead to misreading. Examples include Psalm 139 and the idea that its genre affects interpretation beyond theology alone. The segment ends with the speaker saying they need to finish by the end of the day.

events.title: Finish Work By End Of Day

apps_results.content:
## Main idea
- Read Psalms through genre and family resemblance
- Patterns shape expectations, not rigid categories
- Genre helps interpret meaning accurately

## Key examples
- Ancient writing uses imitation and variation
- Different genres change interpretation
- Psalm 139 example: legal defense

## Interpretation points
- Same text can mean different things
- Missing genre leads to misreading
- Some types have expected structures

## Closing
- Categories should stay flexible
- Don’t read everything rigidly
- “That’ll do for now”

## Next steps
- Continue with the material later
- Finish by end of day
